rationale.R1.1: The opening paragraph names the copy-on-write multi-version index as 'the central contribution' while the Query/Scheduler subsection reassigns that role to the scheduling policy as 'the load-bearing contribution'; these competing framings must be diagnosed and reconciled.
rationale.R1.2: Several inferential chains require verification: (1) the O(1) amortized random-read claim sits in tension with the later admission that read amplification scales with tier count and touches O(log N) tiers in expectation; (2) the size-tiered vs. leveled compaction choice is justified by an append-mostly assumption whose scope needs checking; (3) the tractability argument depends on a causal link from the storage layer to the scheduler whose logical sufficiency must be assessed.
rationale.R1.3: The 38% annotator-hours reduction is asserted and forwarded to Section 5 with no interim support or bounding condition stated here; the O(1) amortized cost claim is stated as fact without derivation or citation, leaving its evidential basis unverified within this section.
rationale.R1.4: Six prior works are explicitly positioned against: O'Neil et al. (LSM primitives), Delta Lake (row-level versioning), Cassandra (size-tiered compaction), RocksDB (leveled compaction), Steinhardt et al. (adversarial annotation), and Houlsby et al. (BALD acquisition); the claimed novelty of annotator provenance as a first-class indexed dimension needs evaluation against each.
rationale.R1.6: A general section review naturally encompasses writing quality; the section is mostly clear but several claims are underspecified (e.g., 'in expectation' for O(log N) tier access is asserted without a distribution assumption), and the dual 'central'/'load-bearing' language creates avoidable confusion for readers.
rationale.R1.7: Characterizations of prior work should be checked for accuracy: Delta Lake's versioning is described as row-granularity (its actual unit is file/partition-level in the standard implementation); Steinhardt et al. is invoked broadly as covering 'adversarial annotation poisoning' without a specific citation anchor; Houlsby et al. is attributed BALD-style acquisition but the bracket citation is informal.
